title: Morrey Saces and Regularity for Yang-Mills Higgs Equations
speaker: Karen Uhlenbeck
abstract: We start with background on regularity theory for the equations of gauge theory. Morrey spaces arise naturally from monotinicity theorems in dimensions greater than 4. Our main technical result is that functions in a Morrey space which satisfy an elliptic inequality off a singular set of Hausdorf codimension 4 can be bounded in a much better Morrey space in the interior.

proof_stages.label: Singular Yang-Mills setup and Coulomb-gauge ellipticity
proof_stages.summary: The talk reduces the Yang-Mills-Higgs problem to Yang-Mills curvature on Ω\S, assumes finite Hausdorff n−4 measure for S and bounded energy, and explains that gauge fixing to d*A=0 is needed to turn the gauge-invariant equations into elliptic PDE despite not knowing A across the singular set.

proof_stages.label: Four-dimensional removable-singularity prototype
proof_stages.summary: For n=4 the singular set is discrete and known removability follows once a gauge with A in L^2_1 is obtained; Otway's alternative proof uses the Weitzenböck identity and a refined Kato inequality to make the differential inequality for |F_A| strong enough for a maximum-principle argument.

proof_stages.label: Monotonicity as Morrey control
proof_stages.summary: Noether/monotonicity for diffeomorphism-critical Yang-Mills fields gives r^{4−n}∫B|F_A|^2 bounds, exactly placing curvature in the Morrey class with L^{n/2} scaling, and the relevant Morrey-Sobolev multiplication, embedding, and Laplacian-inversion rules are recorded.

proof_stages.label: Codimension-four singular sets from compactness
proof_stages.summary: A bounded-energy compactness argument covers Ω by small balls, counts the balls on which the small-energy estimate fails, and identifies the limiting singular set as an intersection of bad balls with finite Hausdorff n−4 measure.

proof_stages.label: Tao-Tian Morrey gauge theorem and its obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The earlier Tao-Tian theorem supplies a Coulomb gauge with small Morrey-Sobolev norm from small Morrey curvature off a codimension-four singular set, but its proof requires a difficult initial averaged exponential gauge and an implicit-function argument in Morrey spaces where C^0 is not dense.

proof_stages.label: Morrey-space maximum principle replacing the gauge construction
proof_stages.summary: Adapting Otway's inequality, the proof treats −Δf≤uf with small u in the curvature Morrey space: Morrey inversion and multiplication make the potential term perturbative, Stein regularized-distance cutoffs handle the singular set, and supporting eigenvalue estimates give the needed maximum principle.

proof_stages.label: Remaining monotonicity gap and limits of the method
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion isolates the unresolved gap that singular critical points need not provide the diffeomorphism monotonicity used to obtain Morrey control, notes smaller-set and point-singularity cases where this can be bypassed, and tests the method on harmonic maps where the necessary q>1 Morrey estimates fail.

examples.name: Classical q=2 Morrey-Sobolev embedding
examples.note: The speaker recalls the familiar case q=2, p>n, where one Morrey-Sobolev derivative gives Hölder continuity, to make the abstract Morrey embedding rules concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker recalls the familiar q=2 Morrey-Sobolev embedding and states the p>n/Hölder conclusion, but does not derive or compute with that classical case itself

passes.reason: The q=2 Morrey-Sobolev embedding is used as the standard familiar case, not as a failure case or structural outlier.

examples.name: Four-dimensional Sobolev check of the eigenvalue estimate
examples.note: In dimension four the stated Morrey eigenvalue estimate reduces to the familiar Sobolev control with u in an L^2-type space and the test function controlled through L^2_1 to L^4 embedding.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks the eigenvalue estimate in dimension four by identifying the relevant spaces and reducing it to the Sobolev embedding relation

passes.reason: The four-dimensional Sobolev check occurs in the critical/borderline dimension for Yang-Mills and Sobolev scaling, and is used as a consistency check rather than a counterexample.
the four-dimensional Sobolev check sits at the Yang-Mills/Sobolev critical dimension where the relevant exponents are borderline, and it is used only to verify the estimate
The four-dimensional case is the critical Sobolev/Yang-Mills borderline where H^1 embeds into L^4 and pairs with L^2, used as a check of the estimate.
the four-dimensional Sobolev check uses the critical borderline dimension where the Yang-Mills L2 scaling and W^{1,2}->L4 embedding match.
dimension four is the critical Yang–Mills/Sobolev scaling case where the estimate reduces to the usual W^{1,2}->L^4 check
The four-dimensional check uses the borderline critical Sobolev case where the Yang-Mills curvature is naturally in L^2 and H^1 embeds into L^4.
The dimension-four Sobolev check is a critical/borderline Yang-Mills/Sobolev case where the exponents line up, but it supports the estimate rather than refuting one.
The four-dimensional Sobolev computation is a confirming check, and dimension four is the critical borderline for the Yang-Mills/Sobolev scaling.
the dimension-four Sobolev check is used to verify the estimate in the critical scaling case where L^2=L^{n/2} and W^{1,2} embeds into L^4
The four-dimensional Sobolev check is illustrative, and dimension four is the critical/borderline Sobolev/Yang-Mills scaling case.

examples.name: Harmonic maps as a failed transfer of the machine
examples.note: Embedding the target manifold into Euclidean space rewrites the harmonic-map equation using a projected Laplacian, but the resulting coefficient lies in a q=1 Morrey regime where the Laplacian inversion and embedding tools used for Yang-Mills fail.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker tries to run the Morrey-space machine on harmonic maps, writes the embedded projection equation and cutoff/Laplacian setup, then traces why the argument fails at the L1 Morrey endpoint

passes.reason: Harmonic maps are used to show the Morrey-space machine fails to transfer, with the obstruction occurring at the endpoint q=1/L1 regime.
harmonic maps are a standard variational elliptic class, but here they are used to show the Morrey-space machine fails because the argument lands at the unusable q=1 endpoint
Harmonic maps lead to the endpoint q=1 Morrey situation where Laplacian inversion and Sobolev embedding fail, so they are used as an obstruction to transferring the method.
